content:
# Romans Chapter 5 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_005_page_001.jpg

1. Therefore, having been justified from faith, we have peace toward the God through the Lord of us Yeshua Christos,

2. through whom also we have had the access by the faith into this grace in which we stand, and we boast upon hope of the glory of the God.

3. And not only [this], but we also boast in the tribulations, knowing that the tribulation produces endurance,

4. and the endurance [produces] proven character, and the proven character [produces] hope,

5. and the hope does not put [us] to shame, because the love of the God has been poured out in the hearts of us through [the] Holy Spirit, the [one] having been given to us.

6. For while we were still weak, Christos at the right time died for [the] ungodly.

7. For scarcely for a righteous [person] will someone die — for perhaps for the good [person] someone would even dare to die —

8. but the God demonstrates the love of himself toward us, that while we were still sinners, Christos died for us.

9. Much more then, having now been justified by the blood of him, we will be saved through him from the wrath.

10. For if while being enemies we were reconciled to the God through the death of the Son of him, much more, having been reconciled, we will be saved by the life of him.

11. And not only [this], but also boasting in the God through the Lord of us Yeshua Christos, through whom we now received the reconciliation.

12. Because of this, just as through one human sin entered into the world, and through the sin the death, and thus the death spread to all humans, because all sinned —

13. for until [the] law sin was in [the] world, but sin is not reckoned when there is no law.

14. But the death reigned from Adam until Moses, even upon the [ones] not having sinned in the likeness of the transgression of Adam, who is a type of the [one] coming.

15. But not as the trespass, so also [is] the gift. For if by the trespass of the one the many died, much more the grace of the God and the gift by the grace of the one human Yeshua Christos abounded to the many.

16. And not as through one having sinned [is] the gift; for the judgment [was] from one [trespass] to condemnation, but the gift [was] from many trespasses to justification.

17. For if by the trespass of the one the death reigned through the one, much more the [ones] receiving the abundance of the grace and of the gift of the righteousness will reign in life through the one — Yeshua Christos.

18. So then, as through one trespass [it came] to all humans to condemnation, so also through one act of righteousness [it came] to all humans to justification of life.

19. For just as through the disobedience of the one human the many were made sinners, so also through the obedience of the one the many will be made righteous.

20. And [the] law came in alongside, that the trespass might increase; but where the sin increased, the grace super-abounded,

21. that just as the sin reigned in the death, so also the grace might reign through righteousness to eternal life through Yeshua Christos the Lord of us.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "We have peace" (ἔχομεν):** Sinaiticus reads ἔχωμεν (subjunctive: "let us have peace"). The indicative ἔχομεν ("we have peace") is preferred by most textual critics as fitting the context — Paul is stating a fact, not issuing an exhortation. However, the Sinaiticus subjunctive is noteworthy.

2. **v.12 — ἐφ᾽ ᾧ πάντες ἥμαρτον:** "Because all sinned." This phrase has shaped centuries of theology. The Latin Vulgate rendered it "in whom [in quo] all sinned" — implying all sinned in Adam (Augustine's reading, the basis of original sin doctrine). The Greek more naturally reads "because all sinned" — each person's own sin.

3. **v.14 — "A type of the one coming" (τύπος τοῦ μέλλοντος):** Adam as τύπος (type/pattern) of Christ. This is the foundation of Pauline typology: Adam and Christ are the two representative humans. What Adam began, Christ reverses.

4. **v.20 — "Super-abounded" (ὑπερεπερίσσευσεν):** A Pauline compound: ὑπέρ (over/above) + περισσεύω (to abound). Grace does not merely match sin — it overwhelms it. The prefix ὑπέρ signals excess, overflow, superabundance.

— EMET, AI Translator
